refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall establish and maintain end-to-end teleoperation communication with p95 latency ≤ 150 ms under agreed network conditions to enable safe remote operation by tele-operators.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-008 addresses a critical safety and operational requirement. Teleoperation latency directly impacts operator situational awareness, control responsiveness, and safety. The p95 metric (95th percentile) ensures consistent performance under variable network conditions. Refinement clarifies that this is an end-to-end requirement spanning network, vehicle, and teleoperation subsystems.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Measure round-trip latency (command transmission + vehicle response + telemetry return) under agreed network conditions (bandwidth, jitter, packet loss profiles). Conduct acceptance testing with tele-operators performing representative tasks. Log p95 latency across test scenarios.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-008.1: The teleoperation subsystem shall transmit operator commands to the vehicle with latency ≤ 75 ms (p95).
FR-NGHE-008.2: The vehicle shall process received commands and execute control actions with latency ≤ 50 ms (p95).
FR-NGHE-008.3: The vehicle shall transmit telemetry (sensor data, status, video) to the tele-operator with latency ≤ 75 ms (p95).
FR-NGHE-008.4: The teleoperation subsystem shall detect and report latency violations or network degradation to the Safety Services for operator notification.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Implement a dedicated low-latency network protocol (e.g., UDP-based custom protocol) with priority queuing and adaptive compression to minimize command and telemetry transmission time.
DP-B: Deploy a hybrid architecture combining edge computing (local vehicle controller with predictive control) and cloud-based teleoperation gateway with optimized routing and caching.
DP-C: Establish a multi-path network strategy with primary and backup communication links, automatic failover, and latency monitoring to ensure consistent p95 performance.
DP-D: Implement real-time operating system (RTOS) on vehicle with deterministic command processing, hardware-accelerated video encoding, and synchronized clock with tele-operator station.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-A + DP-D (integrated): Implement a dedicated low-latency UDP-based protocol with priority queuing and adaptive compression (DP-A) combined with RTOS-based deterministic vehicle control and hardware-accelerated telemetry encoding (DP-D). This addresses both network and vehicle-side latency sources and provides measurable, testable latency bounds.

potential_sources: Network protocol design (DP-A) is decoupled from vehicle control logic (DP-D) via well-defined message interfaces (Tasking, Telemetry).
RTOS determinism (DP-D) depends on vehicle hardware capabilities; coupling risk if hardware does not support real-time scheduling.
Adaptive compression (DP-A) may couple with video encoding hardware (DP-D); mitigation via standardized codec selection.
coupling_check.notes: Selected DP avoids tight coupling by separating concerns: network optimization (DP-A) and vehicle-side determinism (DP-D) are orthogonal. Both are necessary to meet p95 ≤ 150 ms end-to-end.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Network infrastructure supports UDP and priority queuing (QoS). Risk: If network does not support QoS, latency guarantees degrade.
Assumption: Vehicle hardware supports RTOS and hardware video encoding. Risk: Legacy or cost-constrained hardware may not meet determinism requirements.
Assumption: Tele-operator station and vehicle clocks can be synchronized within acceptable bounds. Risk: Clock drift may accumulate over long operations.
Risk: Adaptive compression may introduce variable latency if compression ratio varies; mitigation via fixed-rate encoding with quality adaptation.
Risk: UDP is connectionless; packet loss requires application-level recovery, adding latency. Mitigation via selective retransmission and forward error correction.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine DP-A to specify maximum acceptable packet loss rate (e.g., ≤ 1%) and forward error correction strategy. Refine DP-D to specify RTOS kernel tick rate (e.g., ≥ 1 kHz) and hardware video encoder specifications. Add DP-E: Network monitoring and adaptive bitrate control to dynamically adjust compression and frame rate based on measured latency and packet loss.

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: Owns the teleoperation subsystem including tele-operator interface, command encoding/transmission, telemetry reception/decoding, latency monitoring, and network protocol implementation. Responsible for end-to-end latency from operator input to vehicle response feedback.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Teleoperation module transmits operator commands (movement, tool control, mode changes) to Vehicle module via Tasking interface. Latency budget: ≤ 75 ms (p95).

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Teleoperation module receives vehicle telemetry (sensor data, video, status, position) from Vehicle module via Telemetry interface. Latency budget: ≤ 75 ms (p95).

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Teleoperation module interfaces with Network Link for transmission of Tasking commands and reception of Telemetry data. Responsible for protocol optimization, compression, and QoS management.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Teleoperation module receives safety alerts from Safety Services (e.g., latency violation, network degradation) and displays to tele-operator. Enables operator awareness of system health.

module_containment_scores.notes: Teleoperation module is the natural owner of latency optimization logic, protocol implementation, and tele-operator interface. Latency monitoring and network optimization are core responsibilities. Minimal diffusion: only external interfaces are Tasking, Telemetry, Network Link, and SafetyAlert.

Vehicle module owns vehicle-side latency (command processing, control execution, telemetry encoding). However, end-to-end latency requirement spans both Vehicle and Teleoperation; assigning to Vehicle alone would diffuse responsibility for network-side latency optimization.

Safety Services owns latency monitoring and alerting (SafetyAlert interface), but not the latency optimization logic itself. Assigning the entire FR to Safety Services would create diffusion and confusion of responsibilities.

NGHE Solution is the top-level system; assigning latency FR here would diffuse responsibility across multiple subsystems without clear ownership. Appropriate for system-level verification, not detailed design.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: Teleoperation module is the optimal owner because it controls the critical path for latency: command encoding, network transmission, telemetry decoding, and operator feedback. The module owns the Tasking (out), Telemetry (in), and Network Link (inout) interfaces, which are the primary latency-sensitive interfaces. Vehicle module owns vehicle-side latency (sub-FR-008.2), which is a sub-requirement.
containment_risks: Risk: Teleoperation module may not have visibility into vehicle-side latency (command processing, control execution). Mitigation: Vehicle module must report processing latency via Telemetry interface; Teleoperation module monitors end-to-end latency and alerts if vehicle-side latency exceeds budget.

Risk: Network Link interface may be owned by a separate infrastructure module, creating dependency. Mitigation: Define clear interface contract (latency SLA, packet loss tolerance) between Teleoperation and Network Link.

Risk: Safety Services owns SafetyAlert interface; Teleoperation depends on timely alerts. Mitigation: Define alert latency requirement (e.g., ≤ 10 ms) to ensure operator notification does not add to end-to-end latency.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Clarify ownership of Network Link module and define latency SLA contract. Ensure Vehicle module has capability to report processing latency via Telemetry. Define SafetyAlert latency requirement to prevent cascading delays. Consider adding a sub-IM for 'Latency Monitor' within Teleoperation to isolate monitoring logic from protocol logic.

fr_dp_gate.reason: Selected DP (UDP-based protocol + RTOS determinism) directly addresses FR requirement for p95 ≤ 150 ms latency. DP is specific, measurable, and testable. Coupling check passes: network and vehicle-side optimizations are orthogonal. Assumptions and risks are identified and mitigatable.

diffusion_risks: Teleoperation module owns network protocol (DP-A) but depends on Vehicle module for RTOS implementation (DP-D). Mitigation: Define clear interface contract for vehicle-side latency via Telemetry.
Network Link module ownership unclear; if owned by separate infrastructure team, coordination required for QoS configuration. Mitigation: Define Network Link SLA in interface specification.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Teleoperation module achieves 95% containment score. Primary responsibility is network-side latency optimization and tele-operator interface. Vehicle module owns vehicle-side latency (sub-FR-008.2) with clear interface boundary. Minimal diffusion across module boundaries.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Latency requirement is measurable and testable via instrumentation of Tasking, Telemetry, and Network Link interfaces. Acceptance testing can be conducted under agreed network conditions. p95 metric is statistically sound for variable network environments.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: FR is clearly allocated to Teleoperation module with sub-FRs allocated to Vehicle (command processing, telemetry encoding) and Safety Services (latency monitoring/alerting). Interface boundaries are explicit (Tasking, Telemetry, Network Link, SafetyAlert).

recommended_revisions: Refine DP-A to specify packet loss tolerance and forward error correction strategy.
Refine DP-D to specify RTOS kernel tick rate and hardware video encoder specifications.
Define Network Link SLA (latency, jitter, packet loss) to establish baseline for Teleoperation optimization.
Define SafetyAlert latency requirement (e.g., ≤ 10 ms) to prevent cascading delays.
Add sub-IM for 'Latency Monitor' within Teleoperation to isolate monitoring logic from protocol logic.

decision.justification: FR-NGHE-008 is not atomic; it decomposes into four sub-FRs (command transmission latency, vehicle processing latency, telemetry transmission latency, latency monitoring/alerting). Each sub-FR requires detailed design and allocation to specific modules (Teleoperation, Vehicle, Safety Services). Proceeding to next decomposition level is necessary to establish clear ownership, interface contracts, and verification criteria for each sub-requirement.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-NGHE-008.1: The teleoperation subsystem shall transmit operator commands to the vehicle with latency ≤ 75 ms (p95).
FR-NGHE-008.2: The vehicle shall process received commands and execute control actions with latency ≤ 50 ms (p95).
FR-NGHE-008.3: The vehicle shall transmit telemetry (sensor data, status, video) to the tele-operator with latency ≤ 75 ms (p95).
FR-NGHE-008.4: The teleoperation subsystem shall detect and report latency violations or network degradation to the Safety Services for operator notification.

assumptions: Network infrastructure supports UDP and QoS (priority queuing).
Vehicle hardware supports RTOS and hardware video encoding.
Tele-operator station and vehicle clocks can be synchronized within acceptable bounds.
Agreed network conditions are defined and stable (bandwidth, jitter, packet loss profiles).
Operator situational awareness and control responsiveness are acceptable with p95 ≤ 150 ms latency (validated via human factors analysis).
open_risks: Network QoS may not be available in all deployment scenarios; fallback strategy needed.
Vehicle hardware may not support RTOS or hardware video encoding; software-based alternatives may not meet latency targets.
Clock synchronization drift may accumulate over long operations; impact on latency measurement and control stability.
Adaptive compression may introduce variable latency; fixed-rate encoding with quality adaptation preferred but may impact video quality.
UDP packet loss requires application-level recovery; impact on latency and reliability trade-off.
evidence_needed: Network infrastructure specification and QoS capabilities.
Vehicle hardware specifications (processor, video encoder, RTOS support).
Human factors analysis validating operator performance with p95 ≤ 150 ms latency.
Prototype latency measurements under agreed network conditions.
Clock synchronization strategy and drift analysis.
Packet loss recovery strategy and impact on latency.
